02 / 04 Fixed-Wing Aircraft Design

Spring
Plane

A simple model RC plane constructed over about 6 days during spring break. The plane was made out of cardboard, a plastic Coke bottle, and carbon fiber rods reused from a previous project. The design was optimized for simple, rapid construction and high tolerance for manufacturing errors. Unlike the summer plane, this one had direct flight controls — but no automatic flight control, inertial measurements, or control algorithms. This made it harder to control, although much easier to manufacture.

Complete
Cardboard Build
NACA 2408
Brushless Motor
RC Fixed-Wing
OpenVSP model — spring plane
VSP

OpenVSP Model

The final rendering of the airplane in OpenVSP.

NACA 2408 design notes
00a

Design Notes

I chose to design my own Kline-Fogleman airfoil (KF airfoil). This design approximates an airfoil shape by using flat sheets, and is easy to manufacture. I did my best to model a NACA 2408 airfoil using cardboard sheets, chosen because its thin profile would be easier to match.

cardboard airfoil section prototype
00b

Airfoil Prototype

A physical cardboard mockup of the KF airfoil cross-section, testing the layered flat-sheet construction method before committing to the full wing build.

T-tail assembly — cardboard
02

T-Tail Assembly

motor mounted in bottle nose cone
motor stator — face-on
03

Motor Integration

The motor conveniently fit on top of the Coke bottle cap — partially the reason for choosing such an object for the fuselage. Screwing it in place was slightly challenging.

wiring diagram — ESC, receiver, servos
04

Wiring Diagram

A paper drawing of the relatively simple electronics required. The last channel actually didn't end up getting spliced to both ailerons — rather the secondary aileron was its own channel and controlled via an inverse slave relationship to the first. In total 5 of 6 channels were used: 2 ailerons, 1 elevator, 1 rudder, and throttle.

rudder servo mounted on wing
05

Rudder Servo

The rudder servo was mounted directly on top of the wing for an easy connective path to the rudder.

electronics interior — Coke bottle fuselage
07

Electronics Interior

A view of the Coke bottle housing all the electronics underneath the main wing. I hoped that having a high-set wing would assist with roll stability. The left aileron servo can also be seen in the foreground.

full airframe on floor — carbon rod nose extensions
08

Full Airframe

This photo shows the final airframe assembly. Notably, there are two carbon fiber rods with duct tape that extend far past the nose of the plane. These were put in place in order to move the CG further forward to assist with the plane's stability.

outdoor pre-flight — motor and prop mounted
09

Pre-Flight

A simple, rough cardboard support was added underneath the fuselage. This let me rest the plane on the ground in a position that I hoped would assist with take-off. Similarly I hoped it would protect the more vulnerable parts of the assembly in the case of an uncontrolled landing.

remote ailerons
10a

Remote Ailerons

A short clip testing the connection between the remote and the ailerons. All channels required a bit of tuning — the aileron directions were initially reversed, and custom intensity paths were set up for each channel.

flight test 1
flight test 2
10b / 10c

Flight Tests

Two intermediate flight tests, launched by hand. Initial tests attempted to launch directly from the ground but the torque from the motor was causing the plane to rotate. Worried about an uncontrolled take-off I decided to launch from the air instead. Controlling the plane was extremely difficult, and I was cautious about hitting the throttle too hard to avoid a serious crash. On each launch I held the elevator in a position to pitch the plane up so that it wouldn't nosedive. This unsurprisingly caused the plane to stall soon after leaving my hand, though it was a relief to see the elevator working.

Summary

Project Summary

This plane was extremely lightweight, which I hoped would help it support its own flight unlike the summer plane. However it was heavily impacted by the motor torque and propwash, which made takeoff challenging. Although it could fly, the wings did not create a stable enough design to excuse the removal of automatic flight control software, which made the plane difficult to control. As a novice RC pilot, my skills were not up to the task of simultaneously launching and piloting the plane. My favorite parts of this project were designing and creating the KF airfoil, and setting up the remote/receiver connection to control the servos by hand.
